turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument next in Case 25-5, Noem versus Al Otro Lado. Mr. Suri.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Respondents' position is that when Congress said "arrives in the United States," it meant stopped outside the United States. That theory is wrong for the simple reason that it defies the statutory text. You can't arrive in the United States while you're still standing in Mexico. That should be the end of this case. But, even if you see some ambiguity in the text, the Court's decision in Sale should clinch the case for us. In Sale, this Court determined that the protections of the refugee convention and the withholding-of-deportation statute do not extend to aliens outside the United States and, further, that it is entirely lawful for the executive branch to prevent aliens from reaching U.S. soil and claiming those protections. If Congress meant to depart from that territorial approach when it adopted the statute here, just three years after Sale, it would have said so. It didn't. This Court should, therefore, uphold the metering policy and reverse the Ninth Circuit's judgment.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Are there treaty obligations that are implicated by either the metering policy or the issues here?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: No, there are not, Justice Thomas. Respondents rely primarily here on the refugee convention and the protocol, but there are two reasons for which those don't apply in this case and those aren't violated by metering. The first is, as this Court held in Sale, those provisions simply don't apply extraterritorially. They apply to the return of someone from the United States, not to, in Sale, the return of Haitian refugees who are interdicted in international waters. And, secondly, even where that convention does apply, what it prohibits is returning someone to a foreign country. Metering doesn't return anyone to Haiti or Guatemala or wherever the person might be coming from. It just says you can't set foot in the United States.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm not sure that I understand how you can say that we're not violating our treaty obligations with your reading and why Sale -- you think Sale protects you. Sale very clearly says that U.S. asylum and withholding protections apply to those who reside in or have arrived at the border of the United States. It also explained that refoulement means a defensive act of resistance or exclusion at the -- at the border, not merely returning a person to a country. Now the Refugee Act was passed in response to the MS St. Louis situation, which was a ship in 1939 carrying over 900 Jewish refugees that landed in Cuba, the refugees weren't permitted to unload themselves. It then came to the coast of Saint -- of Florida, and the U.S. wouldn't let it dock at all, all right, and turned it back. And it did the same in -- it did the same in Canada. Canada wouldn't let it dock. So the Refugee Act was passed to ensure that people who arrived at a border and knocked on the door -- because that's what you're saying. These are people who come to the line, there's an agent standing at the line that's open to everybody else except refugees, correct? They're letting in workers with permits to come into work, they're letting everybody else in, but they're not permitting the people who come to the line, to the door, and knock on it who want to claim refugee status. You're saying we're not going to inspect you. How does that not violate the Refugee Treaty Act or the spirit of the St. Louis U.N. convention?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: To start with the statute, the statute doesn't say "arrive at the door," "arrive at the border" --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, but it says "arriving in the U.S." in various provisions. The inspection provision says, "all aliens who are applicants for admission or otherwise seeking admission or readmission or" -- "to or transit through the U.S. shall be inspected by immigration officers." So various provisions of 1125 also speak about aliens arriving. Someone on a plane arriving to land in LaGuardia may not have put their foot on U.S. land, but they've arrived in the United States. They're arriving. They're knocking on the door.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Again, the statute that is specifically at issue here says "arrives in the United States."

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So, if I believe that your reading violates the U.N. convention, then what you're telling me is that Congress, when it passed this language in 1996, intended to reject its treaty obligations?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: No, I'm telling you --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Without conversation about it, and a House report that basically said there is no evidence that Congress -- Congress, in fact, told us it intended to make that provision. The House report stated that the new version of 1158 applies "to any" -- "any alien who is physically present in the United States or at the border of the United States." That was the language of the House report.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Not the language of the statute. The statute says "in the United States" and not --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But don't we read statutes in their context?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: You read statutes according to their text. And there's no ambiguity --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You start with the text and context, correct?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- Mr. -- Mr. Suri, the text has to make sense. And what is a little concerning to me about your reading of the statute is the practical implications insofar as it suggests that a Congress that was authorizing asylum would be requiring people to break the law in order to obtain it. So imagine a polite asylum seeker who wants to do everything by the book, he approaches the border but does not cross precisely because the law says you are not supposed to enter the United States without authority. Why on earth would Congress have intended or meant for his asylum request to be discarded, not taken seriously, not entertained, but someone who manages to enter the United States unlawfully, when the law says you're not supposed to do it, and requests asylum gets their application entertained? That doesn't seem to me to make any sense, that if we're trying to think about what "arriving in" means, surely, Congress was contemplating that a person was -- would be coming to the United States, would be doing so with an intent to comply with the law that says you're not supposed to enter and thereby asking for entry.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: There are two reasons for which that makes sense. The first is that we have a separate refugee admission statute, Section 1157, that protects aliens outside the United States. So it makes perfect sense for Congress to say: If you're in the United States, you go to the asylum route, and if you're outside the United States, you go to the refugee route.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Yes. But I'm trying to understand your -- the rationality of your interpretation of "arriving in." You're suggesting that "arriving in" means the person who is literally standing on the U.S. side of the border as opposed to the person who approaches the U.S. side of the border. And I don't understand why Congress would make that distinction.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The second reason that Congress would have made that distinction, apart from the fact that the person in Mexico already has this other statute, the refugee statute that he could turn to, is that the problem of the world's refugees is not solely the United States' burden to bear. It's a shared responsibility of nations throughout the world and it makes --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No, I understand. But think about the statute at issue. It says "arriving in." It -- it, first of all, distinguishes physically inside, right, and it says "arriving in." And it gives those people the ability to ask for asylum. And the way that your reading plays out, a person who is coming to, arriving in, approaching the United States, can't make that ask, but they have to somehow violate the law by entering the United States in order to make that ask.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: If I might complete the answer, Congress could reasonably determine that if a refugee is in Mexico, then the Mexican government is primarily responsible for processing his claim. Once the refugee is in the United States, whether legally or illegally, then the United States has greater responsibility toward that person.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I guess I don't --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Counsel --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Suri --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- understand how that's responsive -- can I just finish? I don't understand how that's responsive to my point, which is the person who is arriving in the United States asking for permission to enter is not necessarily "in Mexico" in a way that implicates all the concerns that you're talking about. Yes, he's physically standing in Mexico. But imagine a person who has a placard on their body as they approach the border that says, you know, I would like entry, I'm being persecuted in Mexico. Your suggestion that the United States would say: Unless you can figure out a way to illegally cross, we're not going to entertain that claim, seems very peculiar.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: It seems to me that the premise of the question is that there is something improper about blocking someone from completing the arrival, arriving into the States.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: The premise of the question is that "arriving in" has to mean something.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And to suggest that it means that you have to actually illegally cross doesn't make sense.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: There are other penalties that apply to people who illegally cross. It's a crime to illegally cross into the United States. And the fact that someone has illegally crossed into the United States can be taken into account when the person is making the asylum application.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Precisely.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Suri, I --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Sorry, please. Counsel, the statutory language, is it "arriving in" or is it "arrives in"?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: It's "arrives in."

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Suri, if --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Suri --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I don't think you had much of a chance to answer the part of Justice Sotomayor's question, which I am interested in, about the treaty obligations and particularly how the treaty obligations should or should not be interpreted in light of the St. Louis incident that gave rise to the treaty.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The treaty was a compromise between the interest in protecting refugees and the interest in protecting national sovereignty. And the compromise that was reached was we're not going to require nations to admit anyone into the country, but we're going to forbid sending people back to other countries if -- once they have already entered that country. And, here, the treaty doesn't apply for two reasons. One, the individuals at issue haven't entered the United States, and, two, they're not being sent back to any foreign country. They're just being denied admission to the United States. I would also note that even on any interpretation of the statute, the St. Louis situation wouldn't be covered because there's a parenthetical in the statute that talks about people interdicted in United States or international waters, and they're covered by the statute only if they're brought to the United States. So, in the St. Louis example, those individuals were not brought to the United States. Therefore, they'd be outside the scope of the statute, I think, even on Respondents' reading.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I think Justice Jackson's question got at the fact that your interpretation privileges someone who illegally enters over someone who legally comes close to the border. And why would Congress privilege someone who illegally enters the United States? Can you answer that directly?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: I -- I think it's a bit unfair to say that we're privileging people who illegally enter because people who legally enter are being treated better than people who illegally enter.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I got that distinction. The distinction is between the illegal entrant and the person who lawfully gets very close and wants to follow the rules, that person is disadvantaged as compared -- on asylum as compared to the person who illegally enters. And the question as I understood it is why would Congress do that, so what's the answer?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Two answers to that. The first is we would deny that the person is being privileged because metering is not saying to the person, the polite asylum seeker in Justice Jackson's words, you can never enter the United States and your only option is to enter illegally. It's saying our port is at capacity today, try again some other day, and that time when that person comes in, that person could come in legally. The second answer is that Congress could reasonably determine that the United States has greater responsibilities to aliens in the United States than to aliens in Mexico. That's how sovereignty is divided among nations in the world. And whether you got here illegally or illegally, Congress could say we have greater responsibilities once you're within our borders.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: More generally or separately, I guess, what is the problem -- what was the problem that was trying to be solved here when this policy was put in place?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The policy was put in place in 2016 when ports which might have a capacity of something like 50 or a hundred people, a holding capacity of 50 to a hundred people, were facing lines of aliens far larger than that. And those individuals were demanding entry at that particular time, and ports were overwhelmed. Sometimes they didn't have enough food to feed the people arriving at -- in the United States. Sometimes they didn't have beds in which they could sleep. They didn't have places where they could hold them. So it was necessary for the ports to say, sorry, we're at capacity, try again some other time. And I think Respondents' position is that that is categorically illegal in any circumstances because it always violates the asylum statute and the inspection statute to say we're full right now.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That's not quite what they're saying. It's certainly not what the circuit court said. What the circuit court said was we have a law that permits the United States to have people reside in Mexico or stay in Mexico while the application is being considered. So there is already that remedy for the government, correct?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: There is a contiguous territory return provision, but --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Exactly. And that only -- and what the circuit said is, it's okay for you to say stay somewhere else while we process this. What you can't do is do what the statute doesn't permit you to do, which is not to inspect me at all. The circuit said you can make a list of people and have a rational basis to tell them to come back in a particular time. But, instead, what you were doing was saying you, alien, we're not going to even consider your application, we're not going to take your number, we're not going to do anything. We're just going to turn you back. We're going to be the ship on the MS Louis and ship you back to be killed wherever you end up. And that's what the statute doesn't permit. Now you mentioned --

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Might I answer that?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: That's an argument that Sale was wrongly decided because that's exactly what the United States was doing in Sale, sipping -- shipping people back to Haiti.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, because Sale made very clear that the U.N. obligation is, if you're a refugee who's arriving at the port of entry, if you're knocking on the door and I'm staring you in the face, you have an obligation to at least listen to my application.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: And the --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And you can tell me to wait, you can put me in a safer place to wait, you can do a bunch of different things. What you can't do is just turn me back from the border.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: And the second answer is we're not shipping people back to Haiti or whichever country the person might be coming from.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No. You're just telling them walk back.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: No, we're not telling them anything.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So they can just -- the people on the St. Louis could have swam back. They happened to be on a boat. But that's what we did. They were off the coast of Florida, and we didn't let them dock. We didn't let them dock and take -- and interview them at all. We didn't consider whether they were being persecuted. And the majority of those people were shipped back or had to go back from where they came and were killed. That's what we're doing here, isn't it?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Like the Court in Sale, I do not deny the moral weight of claims made by refugees. But that is not the question before the Court. The question before the Court --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So tell me why --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Could I -- would you complete your answer?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The question before the Court is, what obligations did Congress impose in the asylum and inspection statutes? And those refer only to aliens who arrive in the United States.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: May I ask you a question about the statute and about that answer? You know, I totally get that if you just look at the language "who arrives in the United States," it sort of suggests, well, you have to be in the United States. So totally get that. It does seem to me that this statute -- you don't have to be a superfluity hawk to think that this statute has a massive superfluity argument -- problem, because the way you're understanding that second phrase, the statute ends up saying, essentially, any alien who is in the United States or who is in the United States. So what led to that kind of superfluity in this statute, and how are we to understand it? It really -- if you just sort of -- you -- it can't mean that. So the "in the United States" must mean something else.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: I agree that the "arrives" category is a subset of the "present" category, but there are a few different reasons why Congress might have mentioned that subset the same way it mentions subsets in other statutes that refer, for example, to money or property. One reason is that the "arrives" category is subject to special rules that other applicants of -- for admission are not subject to. For example, they're automatically subject to expedited removal if they are inadmissible. So, when the statute, 1225, is talking about the "arrives" category repeatedly in later provisions, it makes sense to mention them separately in the definitional provision. The second reason is that immigration law has historically drawn a distinction between deportation proceedings for people in the interior of the United States and exclusion proceedings for people who have just arrived in the country. This provision -- in this provision, Congress might simply have been trying to make clear that both of those are covered. The third reason is that under the entry fiction, this Court had previously interpreted terms that are close synonyms for "present" in the United States, like "found in" or "within" the United States, to exclude recent arrivals. And Congress might have wanted to make clear that it's covering recent arrivals as well. So, for all those reasons, the "arrives" category serves important clarifying function.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: You know, it also serves a sort of confusing function. When Congress did this, it must have realized or you would think it would have realized that it was just going to confuse matters to essentially say the same thing twice about an alien who was in the United States. I don't think, on the entry -- what is it called, the entry fiction?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Fiction.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I mean, I don't think you would have needed this second provision to do that because you have "physically present." That should have tipped people off that we're not talking about the entry fiction. And we also have the language about "irrespective of the alien's status," which also should have tipped people off that we weren't talking about the entry fiction. So, if -- if -- I took that to be your primary argument in the brief, and I don't think it really sort of does it.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Well, if you're positing that there are already two parts of the asylum statute that are meant to address the entry fiction, then it isn't much of a stretch to say that there's a third piece as well. Secondly, the two pieces you've mentioned are not included in Section 12 --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: It's like belt and suspenders and, you know, whatever, a new pair of pants.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: And the second point is those two pieces are not in Section 1225. They're only in Section 1158. So, if Congress had included the phrase "arrives" in 1225 but omitted it from 1158, then people might draw a negative inference from the inclusion in one provision and the exclusion in the other.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Can I just ask --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Suri, can I ask you, does the administration -- the metering policy has been rescinded. Does the administration intend to reinstate the metering policy if it's successful in this case?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The administration would like to be able to reinstate metering if and when border conditions justify. I cannot predict in advance what border conditions will look like or what specific policy responses the administration would take in response to that.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So, Mr. Suri, why -- why then aren't we just issuing an advisory opinion in this case? I mean, your back-and-forth with Justice Sotomayor raised another line of questions that I had along the same lines as what Justice Barrett just indicated. At the time in which the district court in this case issued its opinion, the metering policy had been rescinded, and, as you've now represented, the government has no concrete plans to reinstate it. So I don't understand what we are doing other than advising the government in sort of the abstract as to whether or not this kind of thing is lawful. We don't have an actual policy. We don't know who's right as between you and Justice Sotomayor about what metering actually contains because the government doesn't have a policy in effect and hasn't shown us that it wants to have a particular policy. None is on the table. So how do we even have jurisdiction, really, to be addressing this?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The question of mootness turns on whether the Court can grant any effectual relief whatsoever to the prevailing party. You can grant us effectual relief and the case isn't moot because there are three types of prospective orders that the district court issued that are still in place against us, the most important of which is a class-wide declaration saying we can never engage in metering at the southern border. If you agree with us, you would lift that declaration and we'd be able to resume metering. Therefore, the case is not --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But you don't -- but you don't have a -- you don't have a plan to resume -- I mean, are you suggesting that -- I -- I'm -- I'm a little worried about the judiciability in the following sense. At the time the case was initially brought, there was a policy in place, the government was engaging in metering, and, obviously, we had a live controversy about it. But, before the district court issued its opinion, that policy was rescinded. There was no more metering. And yet, I think, in the record, it demonstrates that the parties just sort of said to the district court: Well, it would be helpful if you would go ahead and enter your ruling. That's ordinarily not the case, that you don't just have the district court opining on policies that are no longer in effect. So I appreciate that the district court did and so you would like to get rid of the order. But why wouldn't the right remedy be to just vacate the order rather than having us speak to the merits of it in this way?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: If you'd like to look at the issue through the lens of voluntary cessation, you get to the same result. Yes, we voluntarily ceased metering at one point --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: There are no findings about voluntary -- the district court did not make findings about voluntary cessation, so I don't know how we could get -- go there.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: But the test under voluntary cessation is, is it absolutely clear that the conduct can't reasonably be expected to recur? And that can't possibly be satisfied here because we're telling you we want to do this policy again if border conditions justify it.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas? Justice Alito?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: During the period when metering was suspended, what would happen in the hypothetical situation or not so hypothetical situation you were talking about where a port was capable of handling, let's say, 50 aliens and, instead, 300 wanted to -- wanted to enter? What was done?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: What was done during the previous administration differed from what was done during the Obama administration. During the previous administration, the aliens were generally paroled into the United States. And the current administration has serious concerns about that approach as a policy matter because that means that individuals who may not necessarily be entitled to asylum are allowed to enter the United States without showing that they have a valid claim, and it may be years before the person can be found and deported if that is appropriate.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Do you happen to know how many people were paroled?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: I don't have an exact number on that, but it would not surprise me if it was in the millions.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, the Office of the Inspector General issued a report that said that some of the metering that was done was not done because of lack of space. It reported on the fact that there were empty beds in at least two ports, okay? So Justice Alito assumes the moment in which there were not enough space, but there is a claim that at least one president was using this as a subterfuge for ignoring any inspection whatsoever. Do you have a response to that?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Yes, I have two. The first is that we dispute those facts, and this is at the summary judgment stage, so you can't resolve those disputes in Respondents' favor.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But we do have an Office of the Inspector General report that says that, correct?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: True. The second answer is that Respondents brought two different types of challenges to metering in the lower courts, first, a facial challenge saying metering is never okay, even if done for a legitimate reason. That's the only one before you today. And they also brought an arbitrary and capricious challenge saying, even if metering is acceptable when the port is actually overcrowded, you can't use it as a subterfuge. That's not before you today.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. The language at issue here was passed in 1996. In 1998, the government modified its regulations to define an "arriving alien" as an applicant for admission coming or attempting to come into the United States at a port of entry, which is consistent with the policy that had been followed since 1980 -- '80 I think it is, when the Refugee Act was passed, correct?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Not correct. The Court said in Cardoza-Fonseca when it was talking about the 1980 Refugee Act that the asylum statute applies only to aliens in the United States.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But the port -- the regulations have always been treating aliens who knock on the door at the port of -- a legitimate port of entry, have up until the metering policy, were at least letting -- giving them the opportunity to claim asylum, correct?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: I'm sorry, that's not correct either. The regulation refers to an applicant for admission, and an applicant for admission is --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. We're back in the same circle.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I'm going to take you back to the statute, Mr. Suri, and the superfluity problem. You said to me the second phrase should be viewed as a subset of the first, and I want to understand what you mean by that. What does the first include that the second does not?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: It includes individuals who may have arrived in the United States a long time ago, but the process of arrival has been completed. The person would be present in the United States, but the person doesn't arrive in the United States.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Is that really what you're saying? Is that your best -- because it strikes me that that's actually a better argument than the ones you've given so far, that the first phrase is people who have been here for some time and the second phrase is meant to refer to people who sort of may arrive right now after this statute has gone into effect?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: If you're intent on giving these provisions non-superfluous meanings, then we think that's the --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, that's --

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: -- right way to go.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- a good thing to be intent on, I mean, because this is really not, like, a kind of, oh, like, Congress didn't see that it was doing the same thing in two different parts of the statute. I mean, these phrases are, like, right next to each other, and they must have meant -- you know, if you're right about what "arrives in" mean, I'm -- I'm looking for a theory as to how that just doesn't repeat what the first phrase does.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: It -- it's a subset, but that's different from saying that it has a completely different meaning and --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: -- includes people who --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: You know what --

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: -- don't fit in the first category.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- I -- I got that. But you're -- you're essentially saying that the second phrase is a subset that's meant to respond to the fact that they've just put in a provision for expedited removal which focuses on the same group of -- the same group of arriving people --

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- right? And that -- and that the first provision does not really focus on this expedited removal group of arriving people but instead applies as well, although it also applies to them, and, honestly, Congress didn't really need it, but applies as well to people who have been, you know, sitting in Kansas for the last five years.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: It could refer, for example, to someone who comes here on a visa, the visa expires, but conditions in his home country have changed while he's been in the United States and so now he wants to apply for asylum. He isn't in the "arrives" category, but he is in the "present" category.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Is it lawful to impose the metering policy, for the government to do that, even if a port is not overwhelmed?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: We believe that it is lawful, but we're not asking the Court to go that far today. The only issue before the Court today is this facial challenge to metering that it always violates the inspection and asylum statutes. There are separate claims that it would be arbitrary and capricious to engage in metering as a subterfuge, but that's not the issue here.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. It wouldn't be a statutory issue, it would be an arbitrary and capricious issue then?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: That's the claim that's been raised by the other side.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. And you make an argument, this is a relatively minor point, but it's in your brief, and I didn't agree with it, so I'm going to bring it up, which is page 25, you say that because it's in a parenthetical, it's different than if it had been separated by commas or dashes. Do you really mean that?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Yes, I -- I do recognize --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I mean, I know we have a case in a particular context that once said that, but I don't know that that's a general rule that parentheses are different than dashes in interpreting statutes.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: I -- I recognize that --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Or a comma.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: -- you dissented in the case we're relying on.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I -- yes.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: But --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But do you think that's a general proposition that parentheses are different than dashes or commas in interpreting statutes?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Not that they're different in that sense but that they convey an aside and that material in parentheses or, for that matter, even in dashes don't override unambiguous language outside the parentheses or dashes.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Or commas?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: I -- I think commas grammatically are a little --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I just don't want this -- I mean, I was surprised to see this. And, you know, the Constitution has things in parentheses, and I'm just trying to make sure that this doesn't expand.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The Court does not need to rely on that argument to vote for us.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Suri, when I asked if the administration intended to reinstate the metering policy, I didn't intend to suggest that it was formally moot. And it was my understanding, I just wanted to clarify it, that the injunction in this case still has continuing effect on members of the class because it still matters.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: That's right. There's a declaration that has continuing effect as to the whole class. There's also an individual injunction that has continuing effect as to one particular plaintiff, Beatrice Doe, whom the district court ordered us to allow into the United States. And that's an additional basis for avoiding any mootness concerns.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And that's why the Ninth Circuit said it wasn't moot?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: That's right. And that's presumably why the other side hasn't raised a mootness argument and why, even though we discussed mootness in our cert stage papers, the Court still granted review.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So, under Acheson Hotels, why wouldn't we still not reach at least the injunction? Because Mrs. Doe apparently has represented that she does not intend to return to the United States, and the injunction is specifically requiring the United States to facilitate her entry.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: In the Acheson case, the plaintiff dismissed the -- that plaintiff's claim and told the court that the plaintiff was abandoning her claim entirely and acquiesced in vacatur. There's nothing like that --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So she would have to -- she would have to do more, you're saying, than say, I'm not coming back to the United States?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: I think it's far too late to do any of that now because we also have the class-wide declaration that independently keeps this case live.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Right. Let me talk about the class-wide declaration. You're -- you're asking us to reach the merits of whether or not metering is lawful and reverse the district court's judgment on that basis. Why isn't vacatur a permissible remedy here? I mean, I understand you -- you'd like us to say today metering can be done, give you the okay to do it, but another way to address this, I would think, given that the district court declared a policy unlawful even though the policy was no longer in place, and the government today is saying it has no concrete plans to reinstate it, and the district court issued this order without making any findings about whether or not a live case or controversy existed, I think one could argue that the district court at least abused its discretion by nevertheless entering this broad declaration, so it would solve your problem, I think, if the Court on those grounds just vacated it.

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: No, no.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Why need we get into giving you an answer on the merits of the question of whether or not metering is lawful?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The Ninth Circuit reached the issue of mootness and issued a ruling on that issue. I think it's the first footnote in its opinion. So, unless you determine that that mootness holding was wrong, there's no basis for disturbing the Ninth Circuit's decision. And on mootness, we think the Ninth Circuit got it right. This case isn't moot because we'd like to reinstate metering and we're being prevented from doing so.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But you don't have a concrete plan, I just want to make clear?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Concrete plans are necessary for standing --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: For standing and not for mootness?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: -- not for mootness.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: All right. Let me just ask you one question about your response to Justice Kagan. You were talking about the various reasons why Congress had "arriving in" in this statute and that one of them, you said, was the fact that there was previously a distinction between deportation and exclusion and that Congress was trying to ensure that that was also covered or that the principles related to deportation and exclusion were covered by this new regime. My understanding is that exclusion applied to people who were "at alien border," at a port, but your reconceptualization of what "arriving in" is doing does not allow for at a land border or at a port. And so can you help me to understand how Congress could both be attempting to ensure that those principles were included, but we still agree with your reading of the statute?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: The phrase "at a land border" in this context refers to people on the U.S. side of the border rather than the Mexican side of the border, and we know that in two ways. The first is the Court's entry fiction cases like Kaplan against Tod and Leng May Ma repeatedly refer to aliens on the U.S. side of the border who have just arrived as the --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: In the entry fiction context, but we don't have a case or a scenario in which Congress makes clear that it's responding to the entry fiction when it uses the words "arriving at," right?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: And the second answer is that in Cardoza-Fonseca, when the statute said "at a land border," the Court interpreted -- understood that to refer to aliens in the United States.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Ms. Corkran.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: In the 1980 Refugee Act, Congress established a statutory process for noncitizens fleeing persecution to seek protection in the United States. Congress designed the scheme to track the United States' treaty obligation not to refoule refugees to territories where their life or freedom would be threatened. Because refoulement includes, to quote Sale, "defensive acts of resistance or exclusion at the border," the Act requires immigration officers to process claims not only for noncitizens already present in the country but also for those arriving at ports and elsewhere along the border. For decades, port officers followed the statutory procedures designated by Congress for inspecting and processing arriving asylum seekers. It was not until 2016 that the government asserted for the first time that it can wholly avoid these mandatory duties simply by blocking asylum seekers just as they are about to step over the port threshold. Petitioners' theory of the statutory text isolates the word "in" at the expense of making the rest of the statute nonsensical. By Petitioners' account, the phrase "arrives in the United States" has no meaning not already covered by "present in the United States," "arrives" means "already arrived," and amendments that Congress intended to encourage noncitizens to lawfully seek admission, in fact, did the opposite, permitting border officers to effectively eliminate access to asylum at ports. Petitioners' position is also at odds with the original public meaning of the provisions reflected in the government's longstanding inspection practices -- practices at the time of IIRIRA's enactment and in the government's own regulations, which have recognized for nearly 30 years that the Act's inspection and processing duties apply to noncitizens "coming or attempting to come" into the United States at a port of entry. As this Court observed in Thuraissigiam, Congress carefully crafted our asylum system to ensure that the United States lives up to its ideals and its treaty obligations towards noncitizens fleeing persecution. The turn-back policy flouted both. And the nine -- Ninth Circuit was correct to deem it a withholding of the government's statutory mandates. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: How would you distinguish this case from Sale?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah. This case is distinguishable from Sale in a number of ways. One, Sale involved the withholding provision alone. It didn't involve the statutory mandates that are at issue here in 1225, the inspection and processing mandates. So, here, we have a 706(1) claim about whether the government has withheld compliance with those mandates. Sale is also different from an extraterritoriality and a treaty perspective because it involved interdiction on the high seas, which, as Sale explained, is outside of the jurisdiction of the United States. So it raises a host of questions that aren't raised here. I'd be happy to address some of the points my friend made. I'll --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Could you address 1157?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. So 1157 is the refugee program. My friend takes the position that our -- that we have collapsed the distinction between the two. Our position reflects the way that 1157 and 1158 operated from 1980 to 2016. So, for 36 years, no one was concerned about the distinction being collapsed, and that's because they served two entirely different functions. Section 1157's refugee program is entirely elective. The president chooses whether to admit refugees. It's not something that refugees in other countries can apply for. It's decided between the countries. There are a host of benefits you get if you were admitted through that program. But 1157 is not the provision that implements the Article 33 obligations. That happens through the asylum processing that's under 1225 and 1158. And that asylum processing is also how you determine whether someone is entitled to withholding.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Suri gave some responses to Justice Kagan in saying that there's a subset of people who are arriving in the U.S. who are -- as opposed to longstanding people. The problem with that is those people who are arriving, he says, are present in the U.S. So I'm not sure how his answer gets you around --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- the surplus problem.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes, I agree. My friend made a -- a number of new arguments about surplusage that weren't in the briefing, and I think that is one of them. But I would note exactly what you said. Making it a subsection doesn't mean that it's not redundant. It's still entirely redundant --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, that's true, Ms. Corkran, that it's a subset and so there's still redundancy, but I suppose, if Congress in IIRIRA had just created this big deal about expedited removal and said that expedited removal was what arriving people got, you know, it -- it might be that they just wanted to emphasize that just because you were in the -- that you were subject to the expedited removal procedures did not mean that you still couldn't apply for asylum. So the -- the -- the -- the -- the -- the fear, if you might say, is that if they just had that one "physically present" provision and struck out what had previously been in the statute, the "arrive at the port" provision, somebody might have thought, like, all these people that are now being subject to expedited removal do not have the right to apply for asylum, and this provision is supposed to make clear that they do.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: There is no reason for the inspection mandate to need to make that clear because 1225(b), the asylum processing section -- it's in (b)(1) to (A) -- has a whole process in place for how you assess asylum claims by arriving aliens. So that credible fear interview provision says, if an immigration officer determines that an alien who is arriving in the United States has expressed a fear of future persecution, then the immigration officer shall refer them for a credible fear interview. And then there are a host of provisions that explain how that credible fear assessment plays into expedited removal. So there would be no way that someone could read the entirety of 1225 and be concerned about how it applied to arriving asylum seekers.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Can I ask you a question? I feel like there's some slippage between "arriving" and "arrives." And as the Chief Justice pointed out, the language is "arrives in." I mean, "arriving" sounds more in the process of. "Arrives in" sounds more like you've reached your destination. How do you know under your theory when the person is close enough that we could say they've -- they have "arrives in" or "arrived" in the destination? I mean, what if there's a queue and they're far back, or what if they arrive not at a port of entry? How close do you have to be to the border? Could you say that someone arrives in the United States if they're at a portion of the border that does not have a port of entry? Like, what is it -- if it's not crossing the physical border, what is the magic thing or the dispositive thing that we're looking for where we say, ah, now that person we can say arrives in the United States?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: So I'm going to try and keep that in order in my head, and I'm going to start with --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: -- the first point, which is, actually, it's the -- "arrives in" is the tense that triggers the inspection under 1225(8), but "is arriving in" is the language that triggers the credible fear interview. So that's the provision under 1225(b) that mandates asylum processing within the context of expedited -- expedited removal. And as the government acknowledges and I think Your Honor just said, "is arriving in" does suggest a process. And, in fact, that's exactly -- that determination is what's happening under the turn-back practices. So they have immigration officers standing at the border asking arriving noncitizens, what's the basis for your admission? They get right up to the borderline. And if they say, I'm here seeking asylum, then the border officer turns them away, when, in fact, 1225(b) says that if the immigration officer determines that the person is seeking asylum, he shall refer that person to a credible fear interview. So that argument stands separate from the inspection mandate. And what used to happen is that you have inspection and that's how you'd find out someone had a fear, and then that would trigger the credible fear interview. But both of those provisions are at play here. I'm sorry.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And can you answer the question about what is the dispositive thing that has to happen to say that someone arrives in?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. So a person arrives in the United States at a port of entry when they are at the threshold of the port's entrance about to step over. So they are arriving there. I think that's consistent with ordinary meaning. "I arrive at my house" or "I arrive in my yard" when I'm going through the gate. Now that process of arriving is interrupted by the border officer physically blocking them from completing the arrival such that the person never arrived, but the process of arriving is beginning at that moment.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And is that just for the person? I mean, if there's a -- if there's a line, you know, and you're far back and you hear, oh, no, no, no, we can't cross the border today, have they arrived in and been turned away, or is it only the person initially told, and how could it be something different if you arrive at a port of entry versus if you cross the border or you arrive on the other side of the border, the Mexican side of the border, but, say, cross into Texas or try to cross into Texas, where there's not a port of entry?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah. So, to start with the hypothetical where you have a line --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: -- I'm going -- I think that the -- I mean, our position is not that "arrives in the United States" is doing no work at all. The question is what "arrives in the United States at a port of entry" means. And I think that means you have to be at the threshold. If I'm in a line waiting to get into my house for some reason into my yard and I'm half a block back, I haven't actually started the process of arriving in because I'm in a line waiting to get there. So I think, you know, if -- if the word was being passed down a line that's going over the bridge and people are turning away without actually getting to the threshold, they wouldn't fall under the -- under the statutory inspection mandate or processing mandate. The second question I think has to do with how arrival looks between ports. As a practical matter, the borderline isn't generally delineated in a way between ports that would allow for this kind of discernment about have you reached the threshold of the port.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But doesn't the statute say whether or not at a port of entry?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes, yes. So I was going to get to that part next. So we -- we take out "at the port of entry" when we're talking between ports and the question is, what does it mean to arrive in the United States? I think you would have to be at a place where you could, in fact, cross. So, if you got up to, let's -- let's assume it's a place on the border where there is an actual line, identifiable line. I would say the same analysis applies, right? Have you made it to the threshold and you're about to step over?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: So anybody at the water's edge of the Rio Grande on the Mexican side has arrived in?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: No, the border is halfway through the water.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. All right. So half -- they've got -- they've got one step short of halfway through. They've -- they've arrived, but somebody who's on the water's edge has not arrived?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: I think that's right. So you're looking at being at the threshold of when you're going to cross the border.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: And so somebody who's at the fence has arrived in, but somebody a step away from the fence has not arrived in?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Well, so the fence, if we're talking about the border wall --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: -- that is -- the border wall is entirely on the interior, so, in fact, anyone who gets to the border wall --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: They're already in?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: -- is also physically present in the United States.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: So -- so anybody who's arrived at the fence is in?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: They're -- yes. But that's just a matter of geography that they're -- they're already present.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: To take the hypothetical, though, what if there was a border wall --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Fence along the line, yeah.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Exactly, on the -- on the borderline.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: There, the analogy that I think of is a stadium. So I'm arriving at a stadium when I'm at the entrance going through the turnstile. I probably wouldn't describe myself as arriving in the stadium if I'm still in the perimeter making my way to a place where I can enter. So I don't think someone who's at a --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: So, at the top -- top of the wall, they're in, but if they're at the bottom of the wall, they're -- they're out?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Oh, so now they're -- they've climbed?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah. No, I'm just trying to understand what it means.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah. So I suppose --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: We're going to get these case -- you know --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: -- we're going to get case -- as soon as we decide what it means, we're going to get cases.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Well, so, as a practical matter, that's not going to be a concern because the wall is, in fact, within the perimeter.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I -- I understand. Okay.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: A hypothetical wall that was on the line and a person had kind of gotten to the top and was --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: -- about to fall over, I guess, would potentially be arriving. It would depend on the situation.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah, be in. How come somebody who's in the line isn't in? I mean, if the whole point is to make sure that people who are attempting to get into the country have -- have the opportunity to file asylum claims and they've made it all the way, why -- why does it matter he's second in line?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: I think that's just a function of arriving in or is arriving.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: So the whole debate boils down to the one person who's at the port, the one person who's at the front of the line?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah, and I think that's because I think it's important to remember --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: So the rest of the case you agree with the government on?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: And everybody else in the line -- everybody else who's being metered doesn't have an asylum claim, just the person who's --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: They have an asylum claim. They have to get up to the border to present it. Now I think maybe the question is if the government was stopping people from getting physically to the line because, if you're waiting in line and you just decide to abandon waiting and you don't actually make it to the threshold --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: No, no, no, no. These -- these people want to make these claims. They're there.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah. Right. So, if they --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: And they're being metered and they're told no, not today. And you're saying the person who's made it up to the -- to the inspection officer gets to make that claim and everybody else who's been waiting there maybe for hours or days doesn't.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Well, they just then, once they get up there, can -- can make the claim. That's when they tell --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: But they have to get up there, yeah. Okay.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah, but I -- there's no reason to think they wouldn't be able to do that. And I think it's important to remember that from 1917 to 2016, 99 years, there weren't -- there was not a single example of a turn-back. So what was happening is people would come through the port. At that point when they were in the port, they would be inspected and processing would happen. So it's an unusual scenario we have here where we have the officer standing there and turning people back.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And now we have these expedited removal processes at the border.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So many of them are not getting in at all. But they're coming to the line and they're at least being questioned and permitted to make the claim?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Under the expedited -- if they -- if they were being applied the way that they're supposed --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I think your point that you're making is the statute is very clear that anyone in the U.S., now the other side says a foot. I don't know why the foot is magical. I could put my hand through or my nose through. I don't know. Why does a foot count as opposed to a piece of your body count? So, if you're up on the top of this wall, that's not on the U.S. side? But my point is, in asking or clarifying this question, is "arriving at the border" means you're knocking on the door, correct?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. That is what it has meant for -- for 99 years before this --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Suri suggested that's not what it meant, that that's not what the federal regulation said, that that's not what practice was. Was he simply wrong about that? I --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- in reading the papers, I understood that up until 2016, anyone who knocked on the door had the opportunity to make the claim, and they weren't guaranteed success, but they could make the claim.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: That's right. So the government purports to cite a few earlier cases which it says support its position. That's Leng May Ma, Kwong Hai Chew, Mezei, the Ellis island case. All of the cases that the government is citing it cites are playing fast and loose with entry. So each one of those noncitizens was present in the United States, arrives in the United States, at which point they were subject to exclusion proceedings. That's the statutory process that you got if you arrived and then you weren't actually admitted or physically present in the country. That is all that -- we're just looking for the statutory process to be applied to our class members or we were when this policy was in effect.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Ms. Corkran --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I just want to make sure I understand exactly. There's a line of 50 people trying to get in. The first one knocks on the door, so maybe he or she is processed. I don't know how many people are there processing. But, if there's one person there, maybe Person 3 to 50, they don't have a claim, right?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. So -- so --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: No, no, wait. Yes or no? They -- do they have a claim or not?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: They are going to have a -- it's -- it's going to depend on whether it's a reasonable delay.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, what do you think a reasonable delay is?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Well, so that's under 706(1). I think Justice Sotomayor made the point earlier that the Ninth Circuit made a determination that this was complete withholding because there was no ability to wait. There is no ability to put your name on any sort of meaningful list that would ensure that you were going to --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So, if you -- so it's not knocking on the door. It's getting your name on a list?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah. So it's a -- yeah, it's a turnstile if you're at a land port and it's a -- there's a plaque halfway across the bridge, so not a physical door, but yes. So, when you go through the line, if it's working the way it should, you should make your way up to the borderline, at which point you come across and you're processed.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but -- but -- maybe I just don't understand it. I mean, it depends kind of on how long the line is, right?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. There were no -- there were non-examples prior to the turn-back policy and even during the turn-back policy of there being kind of lines of people waiting to be processed who were outside of the United States because, under the prior practice, you came through the turnstile and then you were inspected and processed. Now there could have been long waits within the port. That certainly happened. And that would have been subject to kind of a reasonable delay analysis. But this idea of kind of lines and people piling up on the Mexican side of the border didn't happen until the turn-back policy was put in place and you've got this bottleneck of people waiting on that side.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, I -- I understand that, but I -- I'm still struggling with the Chief's question. And we do have situations where there are lines. And I had thought you had said to me and -- and Justice Sotomayor and others that the first person at the turnstile with -- with the inspection officer has arrived, but people further back in the line haven't. And now I -- I -- I thought I heard a different answer to the Chief Justice, and I'm just wondering, I really do -- whatever you think it is, I want to know what it is.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: I think I was skipping ahead when I answered the Chief's question to kind of the nature of the 706(1) claim and the difference between withholding --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I'm just curious --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: -- what "arrives in" means.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: "Arrives in" means they reached their threshold. I didn't mean to create confusion about that when I was answering the --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. So it's the person at --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: -- the turnstile, it's the person halfway through the Rio Grande, not somebody two people back, not one person back, not one person who can't reach across the halfway point of the Rio Grande? None of them?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: You have to reach the threshold in order to be subject to the 1225 mandates --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: -- for inspection purposes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So how does that fit, Ms. Corkran, with 1225(b)(1)(A)(i), which says, if an immigration officer determines that an alien who's arriving in the United States is inadmissible, the officer shall order the alien removed from the United States? Now, to me, that suggests not -- you remove somebody from the United States when they are all -- when they already have crossed the threshold.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. That --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Not when they are knocking at the door. So that suggests to me that "arriving in" suggests that they've already crossed.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: So it's a process of arrival. So the person is arriving in when they are about to step over the threshold. And, again, I may go to the plain meaning, and I think about kind of being in a house or a yard. If my husband called and said, are you arriving at the house as I'm walking through the door and I said no, that would be a strange answer to give him. The ordinary meaning is, yes, I am crossing through --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, but if you're -- it's arriving in, the officer shall order the alien removed from the United States, that seems to suggest that your view of plain meaning, whether it is plain or not, is not the statutory meaning because, if you're removing somebody from the United States, that person has clearly made it over to the United States. You don't remove somebody from the United States when they're just knocking at the door.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Well, so I think that provision makes sense if you remember that removal is a legal term. It refers to removal proceedings. So what that provision is assuming is the completion of arriving because, in 1996, when Congress was drafting the language, it had no reason to think that 20 years later, the government was going to change its position and start blocking people while they were in the process of arrival. So you see in the regulations and I think also in Sale kind of the understanding that the process of arrival starts when the person is at the threshold and it continues as they step over the -- the threshold and are being inspected.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: So I don't think there's an inconsistency there.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- so, to -- so, to clarify, when the person is arriving at the threshold, the process of inspection, you say, kicks in based on their arrival, and that process could take place on U.S. soil, presumably without metering. The inspectors are on the U.S. side, and so they're asking and they're investigating, and the person is in the U.S. at that point. But the obligation to do that kind of inspection, I think you're saying, kicks in based on the person's arrival.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes, that's exactly right, and it makes sense when you remember that that is how it had worked from 1917 to 1996, when Congress was -- was amending the provisions in IIRIRA.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Can you speak of --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: It will be interesting -- it'll be interesting to read the actual transcript of the oral argument because I -- I believe that both you and Justice Sotomayor and Justice Jackson on several occasions have used the phrase "arriving at." I think you said you arrived at your house. But that's not the term that is in the statute. Do you think there is no difference between arriving at a location and arriving in the location?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. Thank you for the opportunity to clarify. So we have "in the United States" "at a port of entry." I can explain the amendment history on how we ended up with the prepositional phrases in that order, but I think my first-order answer is "in" is just how you describe being in a region. You wouldn't say "at the United States." You would say "in the United States." I'm arriving in Baltimore when I'm on the train as it's coming in. I am at Penn Station when I'm in New York. So that's the deference -- difference in prepositional phrases, which, even if it doesn't completely answer the question, does give you some pause to say, well, maybe "in" isn't doing the work that the government is suggesting here because it has to do with kind of the natural way that we talk. But the way we ended up with that order of the prepositional phrases is that the pre- --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, I'm not quite sure I understand that. So there's been talk about knocking at the door. Do you think someone who comes to the front door of a house and knocks at the door has arrived "in" the house? The person may have arrived "at" the house.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: No, but that's past tense. Are they arriving in the house?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Has -- does a person arrive in the house when the person is not in the house and is knocking at the door asking to be admitted to the house?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes, I think, here, the door is open. The officer is standing on the other side of the threshold. The person gets there and is ready to -- to take the step over, right? The officers and the asylum seekers here were toe to toe. This was happening right at the line, and they're about to step over. They are arriving, present tense. Once they're inside, they've arrived, past tense, but we know that Congress used the present tense here.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, it does make -- I gather you think it makes a difference whether there -- there's a door or a turnstile, you have to be there. If you're at the end of a long line, you're not there. You haven't arrived at the turnstile.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Okay. So then does it matter how many people are processing the arrivals? I mean, how quickly the line's going to move? I mean, it strikes me as a very significant factual question. In other words, it's not -- if you're at the end of the line, you're -- you're not covered by the requirement to process, is that right?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. So, once you get to the -- you have to arrive, and that's just the nature of the -- the language that Congress used. However, if -- if the government was complying with the inspection and processing mandate, people would be going right through the turnstile. There is no stopping or processing that happens at the turnstile under the prior practice. A person goes through, and at that point, they are in --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So you don't even have to knock?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: No, there was no knocking. You went through. There was no immigration officer who was stopping you. You're going straight through the open door. You are now in the inspection area. You're in the port. And at that point, there would be perhaps a delay of processing for the reason you say. That delay would be subject to 706(1), unreasonable delay. Here, we had a complete withholding. But I think -- I think maybe where we're getting stuck is kind of the idea that processing is happening at the border. That only happens under the turn-back policy. That's the only situation in which you're going to have immigration officers interacting with asylum seekers on the other side of the border.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So, historically, if you were at the end of the line, you still went through?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: There -- there was no line. People just -- it was a constant --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right, there was no line.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. That --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: This only became an issue, is what you're saying, and so the "arrives in" wouldn't have had the significance in 1996, is what -- based on the historical practice. And now we're trying to figure out what it means when there's a new practice not envisioned by Congress?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes, that's right. And if I could go back to something you said earlier, which is the logic or the logical problem the government has with treating this -- kind of the change in language as if it suddenly substantially narrowed access to asylum at the ports. Not only is that illogical, but it defies the express purpose that the text was seeking to achieve, to quote Justice Scalia. We know that the reason that Congress amended 1225(a)(1) was to expand the category of applicants to include people who were already present in the United States and people who -- who entered between ports because it needed to put them on equal footing. So to read that language as then re-disadvantaging people who attempt to enter lawfully makes no sense at all.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Can I ask you, this -- this is a new practice. Justice Kavanaugh just said a new practice not envisioned by Congress. The Chief Justice says we have a significant factual question here about how this actually works. Why wouldn't we wait until we had an actual policy with real facts in the record regarding what's going on? I mean, even if this is not technically non-justiciable, even if there is jurisdiction in the literal sense, we do have some control over whether or not we reach and discuss issues. And it just seems to me that we have a lot of hypotheticals regarding how this policy may have worked in the past, how it's possibly going to work in the future, but we don't have a policy in effect right now --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- that we can actually rule upon. So how should we take that into account as we think about this?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. Thank you, Your Honor. So, as we explained in pages 15, I think, to 21 of our brief in opposition, this case has no virtually ongoing significance, certainly not for the plaintiffs. Now the government has sought this Court's review because it wants to preserve its degrees of freedom in the future. Right now, the border is closed under other authorities, but maybe that's not true in the future and conditions change. That is not a reason for this Court to -- to grant plenary review, and the government suggests that --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I mean, if the government were to just bring its own action, I think Mr. Suri has conceded that they wouldn't have standing because they don't have an actual plan in place that they're trying to implement.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: What they're doing is saying: We have this, you know, essentially extant district court order out there, and at some point in the future, we might want to have a policy like this, so, Supreme Court, tell us now whether or not that's going to be unlawful.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. That's -- that's what they're seeking. That doesn't -- you know, in order -- as the Court said in Camreta v. Greene, both parties have to have a live interest in the case in order for the court to proceed. And that explanation doesn't go to how plaintiffs continue to have an interest in the declaratory judgment against a policy that was rescinded five years ago. And so I think it's odd to -- to raise the voluntary sensation -- cessation exception here, where it's the defendants who want to continue to do the policy.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Absolutely. Usually, voluntary cessation is the defendant --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- wanting to avoid --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- the court's orders.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: And the --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: They -- they -- they remove the policy so that the court doesn't order.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Here, we have them pointing to the voluntary cessation as a reason for the Court to weigh in on this policy.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Right. And it's the government that's saying, you know -- you know, the government has control over the extent to which it's most likely --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Counsel, do you think the Ninth Circuit was wrong to say that it was not moot then?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: So the --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: You didn't challenge that in your brief.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: No. So what the -- the Ninth Circuit said that the declaratory judgment was moot, and the dissenting judge agreed. The reason that the Ninth Circuit reached its holding was because of the transit rule injunction, that smaller class, which, at that point, that injunction was still in place and it imposed a number of obligations on --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: -- other parties. So the -- the Ninth Circuit was not wrong about that. But, on remand, the parties agreed to essentially dissolve the injunction because the burdens that it was placing on the parties no longer had any benefit to the class members. The class members had been identified.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So you're taking the position it's formally moot?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: I do think it's moot for the reasons that --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Why didn't you make that point in your brief?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: We -- we wrote six pages of all the reasons that we thought --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: In the BIO?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes, in the Brief In Opposition. It wasn't until the reply in support of cert that the government suggested Munsingwear vacatur. I'll say we don't object to that.
