We didn't address it in our Brief In Opposition because that would be an odd thing for a respondent to suggest. And once the Court granted review, we focused on the merits. But, to Justice Jackson's point about Munsingwear vacatur, we don't have an objection, and I think that resolves all of the government's concerns in this case.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas? Justice Alito, anything further?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: On that last point, the Court said declaratory judgment, it was vacating that. And then, when -- and it's also vacated the preliminary injunction?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: No. So the declaratory judgment was -- is still in place but I think is moot for the reasons that the -- that the Ninth Circuit said. The Ninth Circuit went on to reach kind of the -- the statutory interpretation question, not with respect to the declaratory judgment but with respect to the transit rule injunction, which would only be in place if the, you know, underlying statutory question was resolved in the plaintiffs' favor. But then that injunction was dissolved, so we really have nothing that's before the Court at this point.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Is your position that when a person gets to the border, she has a right to apply for asylum only or that she also has a right to enter?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: So enter in the -- so right to cross, I'll say cross to avoid --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: A right to cross.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: -- the entry. So I think the right to cross is -- is tied up in the inspection and processing mandates that are on the government. So we have a 706(1) claim here. It's based on ministerial duties that attach to the government under these circumstances.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And if the United States figured out a way to do its various things that it has to do without having the person cross, would that be sufficient? You know, like, you could have a Zoom interview or something like that.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah, I suppose that 1225 assumes that all this is happening on U.S. soil and I think for good reason because that's how it already worked. The government's extraterritoriality concerns might weigh against doing some sort of process, but I don't know that I have a stake in -- in pushing back on that way of -- of approaching things.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch, anything further?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Just briefly. You mentioned the -- the court of appeals revised opinion. It said it didn't think the case was moot because of the equitable relief still present, which I -- I -- I took to mean both the declaratory judgment and -- and the injunction. Am I mistaken?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: So, yes, they were both left in place. The reason that the Ninth Circuit reached the underlying statutory interpretation question was because --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Is because they were both in place?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah. Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: On that question that you made a point on about why Congress would give better treatment to those unlawfully in the country, I guess one response to that, I just want to get your reaction, would be we should just interpret the rule in front of us or the statute in front of us by its terms and not try to figure out all the things that might be going on with people unlawfully in the country to try to make it all fit in some perfect world. Rather, we should -- you know, there are lots of issues where people are unlawfully in the country that -- but the only issue before us is trying to figure out what "arrives in" means and not worry about that. Just want to get your reaction to that.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Sorry, I -- I'm not sure I followed the question.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: You were saying that if we interpreted it the government's way --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- that Congress would have been privileging people unlawfully in the country. And my response or my question to you is, well, I don't -- whatever's going on with people unlawfully in the country, the sole issue before us is trying to figure out what "arrives in" means when you're at the border, and whatever the effects of that are in terms of creating -- creating disparities, we can't sort all that out here. All we have to do is focus on the issue before us. So what's your reaction to that?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes, thank you. Sorry it took me a little bit to get there. So I think it's less about advantaging people who are already in than it is about addressing the disparity incentives that Congress was trying to resolve in 1225. So, under the government's framework, if you are an asylum seeker coming to the United States, you should not go to a port because you will be turned away. You need to cross the Rio Grand -- Grande, you need to come in between ports. That is the exact opposite of what Congress was trying to accomplish there. And I know also --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But the answer to that could be better enforcement of people coming in unlawfully. And Congress might have assumed, particularly in 1996, when there was an increased effort to prevent illegal immigration, that, you know, people wouldn't be flooding in unlawfully. That turned out not to be accurate, but that might have been the expectation in 1996. So --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: But I don't -- I don't think that then explains what Congress was doing in 1225. It creates this applicant-for- admission category in order to unify the proceedings, the deportation and exclusion proceedings. What the government is suggesting just makes all of that somewhat nonsensical.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. Completely different question. Last one. This seems very artificial, trying to figure out at the threshold, on the line, in the middle of the river, because wherever the line is, the government's presumably going to stop you on the other side of that line and prevent you from getting to wherever the line is, right?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: So that you're getting across the line?

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah. So, if we define it as the threshold, the government's going to start -- stop you short of the threshold or try to.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Oh, I understand. So --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And if we say a hundred yards from the threshold, they're going to stop you 125 yards from the threshold.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Oh. So the --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: In other words, the point -- I guess the "arrives in" thing seems kind of artificial. The whole question -- the bigger question, it seems to me, is can the government physically stop people before they get to whatever that line is no matter how we define it, and that seems like not a statutory question, more of an arbitrary and capricious question. But maybe I'm wrong. Just want to get your reactions to that. This whole idea, if you're second in line or third -- the government, wherever we define it, is going to stop you when they want to implement this policy on the other side of that line. Do you follow that?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: I do. And so I think that the -- that maybe the factual premise that's inaccurate there, the port goes right to the borderline. So the government understands ports as being kind of that geographic space. So the threshold of the port is the borderline. So you can't really, if you moved the threshold further in, then you have people who are present in the United States; if you move the threshold further out, well, now we're in Mexican territory, and the government doesn't have authority to act there. So we're always going to be --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, that -- okay. So that's a good answer. Does the government -- how far into Mexico -- maybe the SG -- will the government go -- maybe they don't want to answer that --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- to stop people?

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. Yeah, I think, if the government starts going well into Mexico and started --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, the well -- well into, is that a hundred yards? I mean, 150 yards? That's not the threshold anymore. And will they go into -- I mean, again, you might not have the answer to this question, but this -- the whole thing seems kind of artificial to me because the government is going -- if they want to do this policy, they're going to stop you on the other side of the line.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yes. And I would say there that -- so, if we start to have the government kind of circumventing further into Mexico, whether it's 10 feet or a hundred feet, we start -- we move out of the inspection and -- and asylum processing mandates in 1225 and now I think it's a question of just kind of ultra vires acting without authority in another country.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. All right. Thank you for your help.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: We don't have the answers to Justice Kavanaugh's good questions because there is no policy in effect, right? We don't know --

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- what the government is doing.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: I was going to make that point as well. Right -- right now, we have a legal question that's unanchored from any actual practice before the Court.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CORKRAN
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Mr. Suri?

turns.name: SURI
turns.text: Just a few quick points, Mr. Chief Justice. First, this Court should decide this case. The case is not moot because there is still effectual relief that the Court could award, namely, lifting the class-wide declaration and the individual injunction to which the government is still subject. There's also a strong practical reason for the Court to decide this case. Administrations of both parties since 2016 have consistently said that this is an important tool in the government's toolbox for dealing with border surges when they occur. I can't predict when the next border surge occurs, but I can say that when it does occur, this is a tool that DHS would want in its toolbox. It's not something the Court should leave to future uncertainty. We've been faulted here for not having a specific policy in place, but that's not really fair to us. There's a class-wide declaratory judgment saying that this practice is unlawful. And I think the government should not be faulted for conforming its current conduct to what a court has declared to be the law. Once that declaratory judgment is lifted, we'll take appropriate steps at that time. Second, the text of the statute should control the Court's decision, and that text is "arrives in the United States." We've heard attempts to substitute other language that might be more ambiguous or more susceptible to Respondents' interpretation than the language Congress actually used. For example, we heard many references to "arriving in the United States." But the specific language at issue here is "arrives," not "arriving." Further, as Justice Kagan correctly pointed out, there's a separate provision of Section 1225 that says that "arriving" aliens can be removed from the United States in certain circumstances, and that suggests that even the "arriving" category is limited to aliens who have already crossed the border. Even if you think that there is some ambiguity in the term "arriving," though, the correct approach is to use the clarity of "arrives" to resolve the ambiguity in "arriving." You shouldn't use the ambiguity, potentially, in "arriving" as an excuse to ignore the clear word, "arrives." Similarly, we've heard attempts to replace "arrives in the United States" with "arrives at a land border" or "arrives at the threshold" or "knocks on the door." None of those terms are the terms Congress actually used in the statute. Moreover, even when a previous version of the statute said "arrives at a land border," this Court still interpreted that to refer to aliens in the United States. We've relatedly heard a suggestion that the alien arrives "at" the port of entry because the port goes right up to the border. We don't think that's accurate. The entrance to the port might be something like a quarter mile from the border. So, even if you think the question is whether the person arrives "at" the port, the person hasn't arrived at the port. More importantly, the real question is whether the person arrives in the United States. Finally, Respondents' position raises serious line-drawing problems. It's not clear from their position whether you have to be at the front of the line, the back of the line, or the middle of the line. It's not clear whether they're distinguishing between someone who is stopped by a border officer versus someone who is stopped by the Rio Grande versus someone who is stopped by the border wall versus someone who's stopped by Mexican authorities working in concert with the United States, which is something that could happen in response to Justice Kavanaugh's question. I don't think we ourselves go into Mexico, but we could cooperate with the Mexican authorities to stop people from reaching the border of the United States. Our position avoids all of those line-drawing problems because Congress drew a clear line here. It said that if you're outside the United States, you -- you can apply for refugee admission under Section 1157. If you're in the United States, whether you've been here for a long time or you've just arrived, you can apply for asylum. The Court should respect the line that Congress drew.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
